prompt:
You are auditing the following Postgres migration before it ships to production. The SAME statement is SAFE on one PG version and UNSAFE on another. Per persona Step 4 (lock duration risk): cross-reference the lock level with row count AND PG version semantics.

Migration file:

```sql
-- migrations/20260606140005_add_signup_source_with_default.sql
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN signup_source text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'unknown';
```

Context (Scenario A): Postgres version 15, `users` row count ~50M, rolling deploy.
Context (Scenario B): Postgres version 10, `users` row count ~50M, rolling deploy.

(Yes, PG10 is EOL. The team inherited a legacy production cluster they haven't migrated yet. Audit BOTH scenarios.)

Produce per persona Step 1-6: (1) per-statement verdict for BOTH PG15 and PG10 scenarios (the SAME SQL statement gets DIFFERENT verdicts); (2) explanation of WHY the verdict differs by PG version (PG11+ metadata-only ADD COLUMN with constant DEFAULT vs PG10- table-rewrite); (3) per NEEDS-PHASING scenario, concrete phased SQL; (4) rollback per scenario.
expected_output: PG15 verdict: SAFE-with-caveat. ADD COLUMN with constant DEFAULT is metadata-only on PG11+; brief AccessExclusiveLock for catalog update only, no table rewrite. The NOT NULL is also metadata-only because the DEFAULT applies to existing rows virtually (PG stores the DEFAULT in pg_attribute.attmissingval; existing rows return the default on read without a physical write). PG10 verdict: UNSAFE / NEEDS-PHASING. ADD COLUMN with DEFAULT on PG10- rewrites the entire table under AccessExclusiveLock; on 50M rows this locks for minutes-to-hours = production incident. Phased remediation for PG10: Phase 1 ADD COLUMN nullable (no default), Phase 2 batched UPDATE to set 'unknown', Phase 3 ALTER COLUMN SET DEFAULT 'unknown' + ALTER COLUMN SET NOT NULL. The discriminator: a baseline plan that ignores PG version OR scores both scenarios identically misses the load-bearing PG11+ metadata-only optimization (which would NEEDS-PHASING the PG15 case unnecessarily, costing developer time) AND the PG10 disaster (which would manifest as table-rewrite production downtime).
